name: Question Sequencing Strategy

fields.definition_origin: The 'foot-in-the-door' commitment-ladder doctrine traces to Freedman & Fraser's 1966 social-psychology experiment (small request precedes large) and Cialdini's 'Influence' (1984) commitment-and-consistency principle. Modern application to mobile-app onboarding questionnaire sequencing was operationalized by the Cal AI / Flo / Noom playbook (2018-2024) and articulated explicitly by Reforge (Casey Winters), Maja Voje's go-to-market work, and Mau Baron's Starter Story playbook (2024-2025). The 'low-friction-first → commitment-building-middle → painpoint-as-engineered-peak → identity-at-end' arc is the dominant 2024-2026 sequencing pattern, visible across 50+ Mobbin teardowns. Distinct from Question Type Taxonomy (item 27) — sequencing is the ORDER and arc; taxonomy is the typology.
fields.core_mechanism: Operates via four stacked psychological levers: (1) Foot-in-the-door / commitment escalation (Freedman & Fraser 1966; Cialdini 1984) — small initial yes lowers resistance to subsequent larger asks. (2) Cognitive easing (Kahneman System 1) — easy first questions prime fluent processing of harder later questions. (3) Sunk-cost compounding (item 4) — each answered question increases the cost of abandonment, so question N+1 must be slightly more demanding than question N to keep escalation curve climbing. (4) Peak-End Rule (Kahneman 1993) — the painpoint placement creates the engineered emotional low-point (anterior insula activation), and the AI Plan Reveal at the end creates the engineered high-point; sequencing controls both. (5) Identity priming late (Bem 1972 self-perception theory) — by the final third the user is invested enough to declare 'I am the kind of person who...' which compounds the reveal payoff. (6) Counter-mechanism: violating sequencing (demographic-heavy openings) feels like data extraction, not personalization — triggers reactance (Brehm 1966) and abandonment. The user's perception is shaped by the FIRST 3 questions disproportionately (anchoring bias, Tversky & Kahneman 1974) — fail those and the rest of the funnel underperforms regardless of content.
fields.neuroscience_correlate: Commitment escalation engages anterior cingulate (decision-coherence monitoring) + medial PFC (self-perception). Painpoint engages anterior insula (negative-affect anticipation). Identity questions engage medial PFC (self-referential processing, Kelley 2002). Sunk-cost decisions engage ventromedial PFC + ACC (Strough 2008 fMRI). Sequencing's compound effect = layering these activations in optimal order. [uncertain on direct mobile-onboarding-sequencing fMRI]
fields.empirical_evidence: (1) Freedman & Fraser (1966) foot-in-the-door experiment: small initial request (display small sign) lifted compliance with later large request (large sign) by 2-3x — replicated 100+ times in social-psychology literature. (2) Cialdini's 'Influence' (1984) — commitment-and-consistency principle, 50+ years of replication. (3) Cal AI 33-step sequencing (Mobbin teardown 2024): appearance/style preference → goals → metrics → constraints → AI reveal — opens with low-friction preference, places painpoint mid-flow, identity late. Reportedly converts at 5-10x the trial-rate of demographic-heavy competitors (paraphrased from teardowns). (4) Flo period tracker (Mobbin 2024): cycle preferences first, demographics middle, symptoms (painpoint) late, then trial — same arc. (5) Noom (TechCrunch 2022 case study): 30+ step questionnaire opens with eating-trigger preferences, painpoint mid-flow, identity ('I'm the kind of person who...') near end. Noom $400M+ revenue cited as evidence of the sequencing model's commercial impact. (6) Headway (Inside Startups teardown 2024): demo-led + then preference → goal → identity sequence — outperformed data-led variant by 18% D7 retention. (7) Adapty 2026 onboarding teardown: top-decile apps universally use this sequencing; bottom-decile cluster around demographic-first openings. (8) Bem (1972) self-perception theory — replicated extensively. (9) Mau Baron Starter Story playbook (2024-2025) explicitly calls out the sequencing arc. (10) Counter-evidence: B2B/productivity apps (Linear, Notion) use minimal questionnaire — different doctrine; sequencing matters less when questions are 1-3.
fields.measurable_outcome_metric: First-3-step completion rate (target ≥90% — anchoring window), step-level abandonment heatmap (look for cliffs), time-on-step distribution per sequence position (low-friction openers <8s, painpoint ~15s, identity ~12s), paywall reach rate (target ≥60% for 15-step flows, ≥40% for 30-step), trial conversion conditioned on sequence variant (A/B), funnel-cumulative drop curve shape (smooth descent vs cliff at one step).
fields.evidence_strength: industry_benchmark + practitioner_opinion (foundational psychology peer-reviewed; specific app-level RCTs are case-study level)
fields.conversion_lift_benchmark: Cal AI / Flo / Noom case studies: properly-sequenced long onboarding hits 60-80% paywall reach vs 30-50% for demographic-first openings (industry benchmark, paraphrased from teardowns). Adapty 2026: top-decile apps cluster around the prescribed sequence. Specific RCT-grade lift figures unverified at peer-reviewed level for sequencing-only A/B tests.
fields.engineering_tactics: (1) Define the sequence as a typed array in the FSM, NOT individual one-off screens — this lets you A/B-test the order via server-driven UI (item 31) without code deploy. (2) Sequence template (15-step example, Cal AI / Mau-Baron blended): Steps 1-3: LOW-FRICTION PREFERENCES (pick a color theme, pick an avatar, pick a tone) — 0-cost answers, build IKEA effect. Steps 4-7: GOAL-SETTING (target weight / streak / language proficiency / cycle goal) — anchor value of the rest of the flow. Steps 8-10: METRICS / CONSTRAINTS (height, weight, schedule, dietary restrictions, side-effects) — demographic-equivalent but justified by goals. Step 11: PAINPOINT — engineered emotional low ('What's hardest about your current routine?' multi-select); IMMEDIATELY follow with reassurance social-proof punctuation. Steps 12-14: IDENTITY ('Are you the kind of person who...' / 'How would you describe yourself?') — late, when commitment is deep. Step 15: AI Plan Reveal (item 12) — engineered peak. Then paywall. (3) Anchoring discipline: NO demographic question in the first 3 — birthdate, age, gender NEVER first. (4) Painpoint must be followed by social-proof punctuation within 1 step ('You're not alone — 5M people have been here'). (5) Identity question phrasing: 'Are you a runner?' (identity) NOT 'Do you run?' (behavior). (6) Justification microcopy under demographic questions ('We use this to calibrate your plan, not to identify you'). (7) Cross-step stitching (item 14) at every reveal: 'Because you said [step-3-preference], we recommend [step-15-reveal-content]'. (8) Server-driven sequence config: JSON `[{step_id, kind, copy, branch_predicate}]` allows zero-deploy reordering for A/B. (9) Telemetry: emit `onboarding_step_viewed { step_id, kind, position, total }`, `onboarding_step_completed { step_id, kind, time_on_step_ms }`, `onboarding_step_abandoned { step_id, kind }`. (10) Branching: sequence may branch (e.g., user picks 'lose weight' → metrics step asks weight; picks 'build muscle' → asks lifts) — sequence engine must support conditional branches. (11) Rule: max 4-5 questions of same type in a row before changing register (taxonomy item 27 enforces type variety, sequencing enforces type ORDER).
fields.real_world_examples: (1) Cal AI 33-step (Mobbin 2024): Step 1 'Pick your goal' (preference, large emoji tiles), Step 7 'What's hardest about your current routine?' (painpoint multi-select), Step 12 'How would you describe yourself?' (identity with body-silhouette tiles), Step 15 social-proof punctuation '5M+ users like you', Step 22 AI plan reveal — canonical sequence. (2) Flo period-tracker: Step 1 cycle theme color (preference), Step 4 birthdate (demographic), Step 7 goal (track period / get pregnant), Step 10 'Which symptoms bother you most?' (painpoint), then trial — same sequencing arc. (3) Noom 30+ step: Step 1 eating-trigger preference, Step 8 weight goal, Step 14 'When do you eat when not hungry?' (painpoint), Step 22 'I am the kind of person who...' (identity), then trial. (4) Headway: genre preference → reading goal → identity ('Lifelong learner / casual reader') → social-proof → personalized plan. (5) Prayer Lock (Mau Baron): faith tradition (preference) → personal struggle (painpoint) → daily goal → identity ('Daily / occasional / returning believer') → AI prayer reveal. (6) Headspace 2024: 'What brings you here?' (goal-as-preference), painpoint mid-flow, identity ('Are you new / returning?'), personalized plan. (7) Calm: sleep-struggle preference → painpoint → identity → personalized sleep plan. (8) Counter-example: a wellness app that opens with 'Enter your birthdate' on Step 1 — demographic-first opening, predicts high abandonment.
fields.mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile-primary — questionnaire onboarding is mostly a mobile pattern. One question per screen with full thumb-zone CTA is the dominant mobile sequencing form. Web equivalent (Typeform-style): same arc but multi-question per page tolerated; web users abandon less per question but more per session. Mobile sequencing must avoid keyboard-input questions early (drop-off triples on text inputs); web is slightly more forgiving. Mobile uses native page transitions (UIPageViewController, ViewPager2, RN PagerView) for snappy step navigation.
fields.platform_design_language_alignment: Liquid Glass (iOS 26+): bolder left-aligned typography is purpose-built for the conversational question header at each sequence step. M3 Expressive (Android 16+): expressive shape + spring motion supports answer-selected micro-celebration. Both DLs reward LARGE tap targets — preference questions with 4 large tiles align naturally. Forbid system-default checkbox/radio (reads as form, not journey).
fields.category_fit: STRONGEST fit: wellness/habit (Cal AI, Flo, Noom, Headspace, Calm), AI-wrapper consumer (Headway, Prayer Lock), edtech (Duolingo's first 5 questions follow this arc), fitness, mental-health, fintech-personalization. Acceptable fit: dating apps (Hinge prompts arc, but inverted — identity early), some gaming (intro tutorial is sequencing-equivalent). WEAK fit: utilities (4-screen cinematic wins, item 25), B2B/productivity (use empty-state seeding instead, item 21), single-feature apps. EXCLUSION: 'Do not impose 15-step sequenced questionnaire on a category that doesn't need personalization.'
fields.solo_dev_feasibility: 1_week. Define sequence + 6 question types (item 27) + cross-step stitching (item 14). Most complex part is copywriting per step + branching logic. Server-driven sequencing extends to weeks but unlocks zero-deploy A/B testing.
fields.tooling_options: OnboardJS (open-source onboarding state machine), XState v5 (typed sequence FSM), Plotline (server-driven sequence config), Adapty Onboarding Builder (2026), Superwall onboarding paywalls, Lottie / Rive for answer-selected animations, custom React Native + XState for full control. PostHog / Statsig / Eppo for sequence-variant A/B testing.
fields.ai_dependency: none for the sequencing itself. Optional: small classifier (gpt-4o-mini class) to branch sequence based on early answers. Required only for AI Plan Reveal at the END of the sequence (item 12, frontier model). State: small classifier optional for branching; frontier model required only for terminal reveal.
fields.claude_design_application: Instruct Claude Design to model sequencing as a typed FSM array. Prompt fragment: 'Build an onboarding sequence FSM in XState v5 with a typed array `OnboardingStep[]` configured as: positions 1-3 LOW-FRICTION PREFERENCES (color/style/avatar/tone — zero-cost answers), positions 4-7 GOAL-SETTING (target weight/streak/proficiency), positions 8-10 METRICS+CONSTRAINTS (with justification microcopy), position 11 PAINPOINT (immediately followed by reassurance social-proof on position 12), positions 13-14 IDENTITY ("Are you a [X]?" framing, NOT "Do you [X]?"), position 15 AI_PLAN_REVEAL (item 12), then paywall. Each step is one screen, one decision, single primary CTA. NO demographic question in positions 1-3. Painpoint must be followed by social-proof within 1 step. Identity questions must use "Are you a [identity]?" not "Do you [behavior]?". Cross-step stitching at the reveal. Server-driven JSON config so sequence order can A/B without deploy. Wire telemetry: onboarding_step_viewed/completed/abandoned with kind+position. Forbid: demographic-first openings, painpoint without reassurance, behavior-framed identity questions.'
fields.stitch_application: Prompt fragment: 'Mockup a 15-step questionnaire sequence for a wellness app. Show one screen per step at 390x844 with progress bar at top. Sequence to mockup: STEP 1 (preference) — color theme picker with 4 large 152x152 tiles. STEP 4 (goal) — "Reach your goal weight" with target weight numeric stepper. STEP 8 (demographic) — birthdate picker with justification microcopy below "We use this to calibrate your plan". STEP 11 (painpoint) — empathetic header "What\'s hardest about your routine?" with 6 multi-select pills, allow-other text input at bottom. STEP 12 (social-proof) — full-bleed testimonial card immediately after painpoint, "You\'re not alone — 5M+ people have been here" with photo. STEP 13 (identity) — "Are you a [runner / walker / beginner]?" three full-bleed photo-tiles with overlay caption. STEP 15 (AI reveal) — theatrical loader 5s then full-screen plan card with bold tokens stitched from prior answers. Cabinet Grotesk Bold headers 28px, Söhne body 15px, warm cream #F4EFE6, ember accent #E36B2C. Forbid: demographic on step 1, painpoint without immediate social-proof recovery, behavior-framed identity ("Do you run?"), Lucide trinity, indigo gradient.'
fields.ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI codegen typically gets sequencing badly wrong in 5 ways: (1) demographic-first opening (asking birthdate / age / gender on step 1 — feels like data extraction, kills first-3-step completion), (2) painpoint without reassurance follow-up (leaves user in negative emotional state), (3) identity questions framed as behavior ('Do you run?' instead of 'Are you a runner?' — weaker self-perception trigger), (4) clustering same-type questions consecutively (4 demographic in a row), (5) no cross-step stitching at the reveal — generic plan that doesn't reference user inputs. Counter-prompt: 'CONSTRAINT: NO demographic question in positions 1-3 of the sequence. Painpoint MUST be followed within 1 step by a social-proof reassurance card. Identity questions MUST use "Are you a [identity]?" framing — forbid "Do you [behavior]?" framing. No two same-type questions consecutive. AI Plan Reveal at the end MUST stitch ≥3 user inputs verbatim. Output the sequence as an explicit typed array `OnboardingStep[]` and verify the no-consecutive-same-type rule + first-3-low-friction rule + painpoint-followed-by-reassurance rule before generating components.'
fields.anti_patterns: (1) Demographic-first opening (birthdate / gender / age on step 1). (2) Painpoint without reassurance follow-up. (3) Identity framed as behavior ('Do you run?' instead of 'Are you a runner?'). (4) Clustered same-type questions (4 demographic in a row, 4 preference in a row). (5) Identity question too early (step 1-2 'Who are you?' — premature, no commitment built). (6) AI reveal that doesn't stitch user inputs (generic plan = wasted sequencing). (7) Open-ended text input early (kills flow on mobile keyboard). (8) >40 steps (drops past empirical ceiling — abandonment compounds). (9) No social-proof punctuation across 30 steps (homogeneous monotony). (10) Painpoint at very end (just before paywall — leaves user in negative state at the ask).
fields.regulatory_risk: Medium. GDPR Article 5(1)(b) purpose limitation — every demographic question must justify collection. EU Digital Fairness Act (Q4 2026 draft) flags 'pseudo-personalization' as manipulative — sequence design must produce a real personalized output (cross-step stitching), not a fake reveal. UK ICO data minimization. COPPA 2026: birthdate gating must be neutral (not '13+ checkbox'); placement matters — birthdate as the first sequence step routes under-13s appropriately, but must be paired with VPC flow. Painpoint questions touching health = GDPR Article 9 special category; explicit consent required. Identity questions for under-18 must default to high-privacy under UK AADC. False social-proof statistics on punctuation = FTC § 5 deceptive practice exposure.
fields.platform_constraints_2026: Apple Guideline 5.1.1(v) data minimization — sequence must justify each collected datum. Apple App Privacy Report and Privacy Nutrition Label must accurately reflect collection. Google Play Data Safety form must mirror. Apple Guideline 3.1.2 toggle-paywall ban (2026-01) — paywall after sequence must use side-by-side plans. EU Digital Services Act labeling for health/financial outcome claims in sequence headers.
fields.accessibility_implications: WCAG 2.2: each sequence step is its own focusable screen; VoiceOver/TalkBack must announce step position ('Step 4 of 15'). 2.5.8 target size 24x24 css min for tap tiles. Dynamic Type 200%: question headers must reflow without truncation. Identity questions with body-silhouette illustrations need text alternatives that don't reveal sensitive content. Painpoint multi-select must be navigable by keyboard. prefers-reduced-motion: replace selection-spring animation with crossfade. Provide a 'Skip this question' affordance for optional questions to respect cognitive load.
fields.pitfalls_caveats: (1) Sequencing is hard to A/B test surgically — entire arc is interdependent; isolating a single step's impact requires careful experimental design. (2) Cultural variation: JP/KR users prefer fewer painpoint questions (face-saving); US/EU tolerate more. Adapt sequence per locale. (3) Sequence creep — every PM wants one more question; lock sequence length via design doc and red-line additions beyond 15-20 (or 30 for Cal-AI extreme variant). (4) Branching logic complexity grows fast — keep branches simple (binary at most), avoid multi-way trees. (5) Cross-step stitching (item 14) is fragile — if user inputs don't cleanly map to reveal templates, stitching feels broken. (6) Painpoint placement is psychologically risky — eliciting negative emotion has ethical weight; always follow with reassurance. EU DFA may flag as manipulation if not handled with care.
fields.cross_cultural_validity: Sequence arc is universal but content tuned per locale. JP/KR: minimize painpoint intensity, lean preference + identity. LATAM: warmer copy register, family-context identity ('How does your family fit your goals?'). MENA: faith-context identity (Prayer Lock case). CN: shorter sequences (8-12 steps) outperform 33-step in Sensor Tower 2024 super-app data. RTL: progress arrow + selection animation must mirror direction. Identity questions must avoid Western individualist framings in collectivist cultures.
fields.prescribed_action: Sequence the questionnaire as: positions 1-3 LOW-FRICTION PREFERENCES (zero-cost answers), positions 4-7 GOAL-SETTING, positions 8-10 METRICS+CONSTRAINTS (with justification microcopy), position 11 PAINPOINT followed within 1 step by SOCIAL-PROOF REASSURANCE (position 12), positions 13-14 IDENTITY (using 'Are you a [identity]?' NOT 'Do you [behavior]?'), position 15 AI_PLAN_REVEAL with cross-step stitching, then paywall. NO demographic in positions 1-3. NO painpoint without reassurance. Forbid behavior-framed identity questions.
fields.questionnaire_relevance: CORE — this item IS the sequencing doctrine. Question Type Taxonomy (item 27) defines the typology; this item defines the order. Both compound with cross-step stitching (item 14) and progress-indicator architecture (item 29).
fields.aha_relevance: Direct — sequencing is what makes the AI Plan Reveal Aha (item 12) land. Without proper escalation, the reveal falls flat (user hasn't built commitment); without painpoint placement, the reveal has no contrasting low to peak from. The Peak-End Rule (item 6) operates ON sequencing — the engineered low (painpoint position 11) and engineered high (reveal position 15) both depend on sequence position.
telemetry_events: onboarding_step_viewed { step_id, kind, position, total_steps, ad_variant }
fields.measurement_horizon: Day_0 (paywall reach + first-3-step completion are critical) + Day_7 (retention conditioned on sequence completion vs partial)
fields.emotional_archetype_alignment: Magician (full transformation arc — preference → painpoint → identity → reveal is the 'spell' structure), Hero (challenge arc), Caregiver (gentle pacing with strong reassurance after painpoint), Sage (orderly progression). Mismatches: Outlaw (full sequencing reads conformist; minimize identity questions, lean painpoint as anti-establishment framing), Jester (full sequence arc reads earnest; lean preference + playful identity, skip heavy painpoint).
fields.anti_pattern_paired: Pairs against 'survey-style demographic dump' antipattern. Pairs against painpoint without reassurance (DFA dark-pattern flag). Pairs against generic AI reveal that doesn't stitch (item 12). Anti-pairs with frictionless / deferred (item 19) — explicit category alternative.
sources: [Strategic Architecture and Psychological Frameworks for Mobile Application Onboarding (project PDF)](.agent/Deep%20Dive%20Into%20App%20Onboarding.pdf)

uncertain: specific RCT-grade lift figures isolating sequencing changes (interdependent variables make isolated A/B difficult; data is industry-pattern-level)
